citation.title: Electrically conductive laser-induced graphene engraved Janus membrane for membrane distillation

content: A B S T R A C T Electrically conductive membranes (ECMs) show significant potential in overcoming wetting and pore-blocking challenges in membrane distillation (MD) processes. In this study, a novel Janus ECM has been developed by laser-induced graphene (LIG) engraving technique on a polyamide-imide (PAI) membrane, complemented by a silica-infused PVDF layer on the opposite side to create a unique ’sandwich’ structure. The Janus structure in­tegrates the hydrophilicity of the LIG layer with the hydrophobicity of the PVDF layer, facilitating efficient vapor flux. The optimized membrane achieved electrical conductivity of 142 ± 1.5 S/cm, water vapor flux of 11.3 ±0.56 LMH, and 99.77 ± 0.1 % salt rejection. Its underwater oleophobicity effectively resisted wetting by sur­factants and oil-water emulsions. Furthermore, applying a cathodic potential of 3V enhanced the electrostatic interactions between organic foulants and the membrane surface, reducing fouling and achieving a minimal flux decline of 2.45 %/hr over 6 h. Experiments on mineral scaling mitigation revealed that AC electric fields out­performed DC fields. A square waveform at 5Hz frequency achieved over 99 % salt rejection with minimal flux decline for monovalent and multivalent ion solutions, attributed to enhanced electrophoretic mixing under the AC field.

content: lt rejection with minimal flux decline for monovalent and multivalent ion solutions, attributed to enhanced electrophoretic mixing under the AC field. A long-term test with the monovalent salt solution under AC/1V/5Hz(square waveform) exhibited excellent flux with a minimum flux decline of 1.35 %/hr after 18 h of experiment with no significant change in the membrane LIG structure. The in-situ LIG engraving process and the membrane’s adaptability to varying electric field applications highlight its potential for large-scale use.

content: 1. Introduction Electrically conductive membranes (ECMs) offer an innovative approach to water filtration by combining traditional filtration proper­ties with the added benefits of the electrical field. These membranes are often fabricated with electrically conductive materials, such as carbon nanomaterials [1,2], graphene [3,4], and silver [5,6]. ECMs can improve membrane selectivity and permeability while enabling elec­trostatic interactions, electrochemical redox reactions, electrophoresis, and electrolysis, depending on the electric field applied [7,8]. The synergy of the electric field with the physicochemical separation in membrane filtration is evident in mitigating fouling [9–12], biofouling [13–16], and enhancing in-situ degradation of organic contaminants [17,18]. Despite the numerous advantages of ECM processes, developing a stable ECM remains challenging due to issues with long-term stability, fabrication complexity, and cost. Stable ECM must maintain material integrity when exposed to harsh environments, including acidic, basic, or high-temperature conditions [19,20]. ECM fabrication often requires complex synthesis processes and specialized coating techniques, which can complicate achieving uniform layer and mechanical stability [20]. Furthermore, the high cost of electrically conductive materials may limit the viability of large-scale production and implementation [21,22].

Since its discovery in 2014, laser-induced graphene (LIG) has gained significant attention as an electrically conductive material due to its lowcost production and potential to transform various industries, from electronics to environmental technologies [23]. This technique enables the transformation of carbon-containing materials into graphene using laser beam energy, which causes localized heat-induced chemical

Since its initial synthesis, LIG has found applications in supercapacitors [25], antibacterial surfaces [26–28], flexible electronics [29], and water filtration [30–32]. LIG engraving can customize a sur­face from hydrophilic to superhydrophobic by tuning the laser param­eters, which are partially dependent on the material properties [33]. A variety of membranes with different polymers, including polyimide (PI), polyethersulfone (PES), and polysulfone (PSU), have been processed with LIG for use in water filtration applications [32,34,35]. However, applying LIG-engraved membranes is often limited by reduced selec­tivity due to the open microporous structure formed during laser irra­diation. Therefore, advanced surface modification is also required after LIG to reach optimal membrane filtration properties with proper sta­bility, as shown by Thakur et al. [36]. Their study showed that incor­porating polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) with LIG-engraved PES can improve the bovine serum albumin (BSA) rejection by up to 63 % compared to the stand-alone LIG PES filters. In another study, Tan et al. developed a composite membrane LIG PES coated with polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) and explored the electrothermal membrane distillation (MD) performance [37].

citation.title: Electrically conductive laser-induced graphene engraved Janus membrane for membrane distillation

content: oped a composite membrane LIG PES coated with polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) and explored the electrothermal membrane distillation (MD) performance [37]. This study utilized the localized joule heating prop­erty for MD application, where the membrane vapor flux increased up to 119.5 % with excellent material stability. MD is particularly effective for desalination and the purification of hypersaline and highly contaminated water, offering advantages over traditional methods due to its moderate operating temperatures and manageable energy requirements. Like other membrane filtration techniques, MD is prone to fouling and wetting by low surface energy liquids (surfactants and oil) and mineral scaling [38]. Recently, Janus membranes have shown promise in addressing fouling and wetting is­sues, particularly when the feed water consists of low surface energy components [39]. Two approaches have been introduced to fabricate Janus MD membranes for wetting and fouling prevention: creating a superhydrophobic/omniphobic or hydrophobic/hydrophilic matrix [40]. The superhydrophobic/omniphobic membrane matrix has a concave omniphobic surface that resists fouling by trapping air but re­mains prone to organic fouling due to its lipophilic(lipid-loving) nature [41,42]. Similarly, Janus membranes, with a hydrophilic top layer and a hydrophobic substrate, enhance organic fouling by forming a hydration layer that prevents organic matter adhesion.

citation.title: Electrically conductive laser-induced graphene engraved Janus membrane for membrane distillation

content: with a hydrophilic top layer and a hydrophobic substrate, enhance organic fouling by forming a hydration layer that prevents organic matter adhesion. However, they remain vulnerable to surfactants in complex feedwater, necessitating improved designs for hypersaline wastewater resistance [43,44]. An electrically conductive hydrophilic layer on a hydrophilic/hydrophobic matrix can provide tunable antifouling properties by applying an electric field. The hydrophilic layer of such a matrix ensures uniform contact, while the thermal conductivity of the conductive material can transfer heat from the feed solution to the interface that provides the required energy for vaporization [45]. In some studies, electrophoretic mixing was found to reduce the mineral scaling problem by applying alternating current (AC) to an ECM [46,47]. MD was also conjugated with advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), where the degradation reaction kinetics powdered by reactive oxygen species (ROS) can improve by the dewatering effect, greatly impacting micropollutant removal research [48]. These studies indicate that an ECM with Janus properties could help address MD’s key challenges, such as fouling, wetting, and mineral scaling. In our previous study, we developed an electrically conductive silver ink-coated PTFE membrane for synergistic MD and electro-Fenton (EF) reaction and investigated the effect of wetting and micropollutant degradation [49].

content: k-coated PTFE membrane for synergistic MD and electro-Fenton (EF) reaction and investigated the effect of wetting and micropollutant degradation [49]. The Ag-PTFE membrane showed excellent resistance to wetting by surfactant and oil while exhibiting a rapid increase in reaction kinetics during cathodic EF while degrading dyes and micro­pollutants. However, the Ag-PTFE membrane was only applicable for cathodic EF reaction as applying anodic potential causes oxidation of Ag, and the ECM property is sacrificed. In this study, we chose LIG due to its susceptibility to various physical and chemical stresses, and it can be used for both cathodic and anodic potentials.

content: 2. Experimental 2.1. Materials Commercial polyamide-imide (PAI, Torlon 4000T-HV, Solvay Advanced Polymers) and dimethylacetamide (DMAc >99 %, SigmaAldrich) were employed in membrane fabrication for subsequent LIG formation. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, reagent grade, MW = 10 kDa) was sourced from Sigma-Aldrich as a polymeric additive. Hydrophobic polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) membranes were prepared using PVDF powder (SOLEF 6020/1001, Solvay Specialty Polymers) and DMAc as a solvent. Silane-functionalized silica nanoparticles (SiO2, SkySpring Nanomaterials, Inc.) and lithium chloride (LiCl, Sigma-Aldrich) were incorporated into the dope solution to enhance hydrophobicity and pore structure formation. Sodium chloride (NaCl) and sodium sulfate (Na2SO4) from Sigma-Aldrich served as electrolytes. For oil-in-water emulsions, n-hexadecane (>99 %, Sigma-Aldrich) was emulsified in demineralized water using sodium dodecyl sulfate (Sigma-Aldrich) as a surfactant. 2.2. Membrane fabrication 2.2.1. PAI membrane casting The nonsolvent-induced phase separation (NIPS) method was used to fabricate the PAI membrane. A homogeneous polymer solution was prepared by dissolving PAI powder (14 wt%) and PVP (2 wt %) in DMAc, followed by stirring at 350 rpm at 40 ◦C for 24 h. After stirring, the solution was placed in a vacuum oven for 1 h to remove any air bubbles. The PAI membrane layers were then formed by casting the solution onto non-woven polyester fabric using a film applicator.

content: h to remove any air bubbles. The PAI membrane layers were then formed by casting the solution onto non-woven polyester fabric using a film applicator. The casting speed was 10 mm/s, with the gap height set at 130 μm. The fabricated mem­brane was finally immersed in a coagulation bath overnight to remove the solvent from the polymer matrix. 2.2.2. Engraving the LIG on the PAI membrane PAI was chosen for LIG engraving due to its higher carbon content (67.5 %) and excellent thermal decomposition temperature. Briefly, the fabricated polymeric membrane was taped to an acrylic sheet and placed in a laser bed, which was then exposed to a PLS6.150D (Universal Laser Systems) laser with different power, utilizing a 10.6 μm CO2 pulse laser (75 W). The process involved using an image density of 1000 pulses per inch (PPI) and a scan rate of 25 % for all experiments. 2.2.3. Fabrication of the silica-doped LIG-PVDF membrane LiCl (5 wt %) was first dissolved in DMAc solvent, followed by the addition of silane-functionalized silica nanoparticles (2–3 wt %). Next, PVDF polymer powder (12 wt %) was incorporated into the solution, and the mixture was stirred at 350 rpm at 60 ◦C for 24 h. After degassing and cooling the dope solution, the LIG-engraved PAI membrane (PAILIG) was positioned upside down and taped. The PVDF membrane was then cast over it using a casting knife at a speed of 10 mm/s.

Finally, the cast LIG-PVDF membrane was immersed in deionized water overnight to remove re­sidual LiCl and solvent. The flux decline ratio(FDR) was recorded at the end of each daily experiment to monitor membrane performance with time. 3. Result and discussion 3.1. Membrane characterization A polymer must meet several key criteria to be suitable for graphene conversion through laser-induced graphene (LIG) processing. These include a high carbon content, a high decomposition temperature, an aromatic structure with specific functional groups, and low thermal conductivity to facilitate efficient graphitization.

citation.title: Electrically conductive laser-induced graphene engraved Janus membrane for membrane distillation

content: decomposition temperature, an aromatic structure with specific functional groups, and low thermal conductivity to facilitate efficient graphitization. The properties of the most common polymers used for membrane filtration are listed in Table S1 in the supporting information (SI). In this study, we investi­gated the potential of PAI membrane for LIG engraving primarily due to its higher carbon content of 67.5 %, comparable to polyimide (PI) at 69.1 %. PAI’s decomposition temperature (450–500 ◦C [50]) allows it to endure laser power without premature decomposition. Additionally, its aromatic rings with amide (–CONH–) and imide (–CO–N–CO–) links provide a carbon source and promote sp2-bonded carbon formation. At the same time, nitrogen from the amide group can be incorporated into the graphene structure during laser treatment, potentially enhancing its electrical conductivity and catalytic activity [51,52]. The thermally stable imide groups decompose at high temperatures during laser treatment, releasing gases like CO2 and NH3, which create a porous structure while leaving behind a conductive carbon matrix [53,54]. The PAI(M1) membrane was processed under various laser param­eters detailed in Table S2. An optical image of the resulting LIG mem­brane (M2) is presented in Fig. 2A, with a TEM image of the LIG flakes presented in Fig. 2B. Raman spectroscopy was carried out on the membranes lasered at power levels 7 %, 7.5 %, and 8 % with two Z-axis heights (0.24 and 0.25 inch).

content: Fig. 2B. Raman spectroscopy was carried out on the membranes lasered at power levels 7 %, 7.5 %, and 8 % with two Z-axis heights (0.24 and 0.25 inch). The speed and PPI (pulses per inch) were fixed at 25 % and 1000, respectively. The Raman spectra (Fig. 2C) reveal three characteristic peaks: D band (1350 cm-1), G band (1580 cm-1), and 2D band (2700 cm-1), indicative of graphene. The ratio ID/IG and I2D/IG provide information about the level of disorder and layers of graphene, which can be found in Table S2. The ID/IG ratio of LIG, typically 0.8–1.2, signifies a higher degree of disorder than pristine flat graphene structure due to the formation of various non-hexagonal car­bon rings[55–57]. The ID/IG obtained from different parameters in our study listed in Table S2 shows a range of 0.26–0.83. While a lower ID/IG represents less disorder and high-quality graphene, the nonuniform production of LIG on PAI can restrict the choice of laser parameters. LIG formation is uneven at lower power, whereas higher power leads to instability, causing the LIG to detach as a powder. Similarly, higher I2D/IG (>1) means single-layer graphene, while our study shows a lower I2D/IG ratio (0.27–0.73) that confirms the production of multilayered graphene which can be confirmed through the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in Fig. 2D. The HRTEM image illustrates multilayer graphene with repeating hexagonal and non-hexagonal patterns.

Sharp 2D peaks in the Raman spectra typically indicate single-layer graphene, whereas broader 2D peaks, as observed here, confirm multilayer gra­phene (Fig. S2). Based on these findings, the optimal laser parameters were determined to be 7.5 % power with a Z-axis height of 0.24 inches, yielding a uniform LIG film on the PAI membrane. Higher laser power caused the detachment of the graphene as a powder, indicating the importance of parameter optimization. The XRD spectra in Fig. 2E show the different characteristic peaks of PAI (M1) and LIG (M2), as listed in Table S3. PAI demonstrated three distinct peaks, with the broader peak at 20.2◦indicating its amorphous structure. The other two peaks may result from partial crystallization of PAI during thermal treatment or impurities in this polymer. In contrast, the LIG spectrum shows a dominant peak at 25.9◦, corresponding to the (002) plane of disordered or turbostratic graphene, with an interlayer spacing of about 3.4 Å. The broadness of this peak reflects the random stacking and amorphous nature of the graphene layers formed by laser irradiation. A secondary peak at 42.8◦, related to the (100) plane with 2.1 Å interlayer spacing, indicates in-plane lattice order. The surface zeta potential (Fig.

The surface zeta potential (Fig. 2F) shows that PAI is negatively charged above pH 3, a trend retained after LIG engraving. However, the LIG membrane demonstrates a less negative charge due to the conver­sion of PAI’s carboxyl and imide groups into a pure carbon backbone, reducing negative surface density. Furthermore, LIG’s ability to attract and adsorb positively charged ions from the solution contributes to this reduction in overall surface charge. The FESEM image of the LIG surface (Fig. 2G) reveals a dense "forest" of graphene nanosheets formed after converting the M1 (Fig. S1A) ho­mogenous surface to LIG. The C1s region depicts three deconvoluted peaks of C–C/ C–H, F–C–F, and H–C–H bonds at 284.72eV, 289.34eV, and 286.92eV, respectively. Evaluation of membrane performance in water permeation and salt separation The LIG membrane (M2) should be integrated with either the M3 or M4 membrane to fabricate a complete Janus membrane with advanced water filtration and electrochemical capabilities. content: F.R. Omi et al. Journal of Membrane Science 722 (2025) 123916 exhibit Janus structures, with one side composed of LIG and the other coated with a PVDF-SiO2 composite. Experiments were conducted on these four membranes with the LIG surface exposed to the hot feed water and the PVDF-SiO2 side in contact with the cold permeate water. Fig. 5G presents the water flux and salt rejection of the M5-1 and M5-2 mem­branes, showing an initial flux of approximately 7–8 LMH. The flux in­creases during the first 2 h before gradually declining over time. These membranes have an approximate thickness of 300 μm, with a 130 μm PAI layer on one side and a PVDF layer on the other. The initial flux increase can be attributed to the hydrophilicity of the PAI layer, which enhances water permeation for the first 2 h. The subsequent gradual decline in flux suggests that the hydrophilic PAI layer undergoes wetting over time, enhancing temperature polarization (TP) across the mem­brane. In designing Janus MD membranes, an ultra-thin hydrophilic layer is typically preferred to maximize water vapor transport while minimizing thermal and mass transfer resistances [60]. A thicker hy­drophilic PAI layer introduces greater thermal resistance, impacting the temperature gradient essential for vapor flux and increasing mass transfer resistance, which can reduce overall efficiency.

To address these challenges, the M6 membranes were fabricated with four different thicknesses and varying SiO2 contents to systematically evaluate their effects on water permeation and salt separation performance, aiming to optimize the balance between flux and efficiency. The LIG layer thickness was approximately 25 μm for all membranes, while the PDVF layer thickness varied. As shown in Fig. 5H, the M6-1 membrane exhibited a water flux of 8.3 ± 0.5 LMH at a thickness of 250 μm, with a gradual decline in flux as thickness increased. NaCl rejection improved with increased membrane thickness, highlighting the trade-off relationship between water flux and salt rejection. The M62 membrane, at 250 μm thickness, demonstrated water flux and salt rejection comparable to the M6-1 membrane. However, at 300 μm, the flux increased to 11.3 ± 0.56 LMH with a salt rejection rate of 99.77 ±0.1 % after 6 h of operation. Beyond 300 μm, further increases in thickness to 350 μm and 400 μm resulted in reduced water vapor flux but enhanced salt rejection. Fig. S7 illustrates the cross-sectional and top morphologies of the M6-2 membranes with varying thicknesses, revealing significant differences in pore structure and distribution. At 250 μm, the absence of top-layer pores likely accounts for the lower water flux.

In contrast, at 300 μm, the presence of visible finger-like pores and top-layer pores improved both flux and salt rejection, with the increased distance between the pore tips and the bottom layer pre­venting membrane wetting. At 350 μm and 400 μm, longer finger-like pores with larger top-layer pores were observed, potentially leading to wetting and reduced flux. 3.2.2. Membrane performance evaluation for surfactant and oil-water emulsion The 0.5 mM SDS concentration in a 3.5 % NaCl solution was selected as it corresponds to the critical micelle concentration (CMC), as reported by Kharraz et al. [61] with a surface tension of 27.66 ± 0.53 mN/m. Adding 1 g/L n-hexadecane oil to this solution further reduced the surface tension to approximately 19.92 ± 0.01 mN/m, increasing the likelihood of pore wetting and blocking. As shown in Fig. S8, the M3 and M4 membranes exhibited rapid wetting upon introducing SDS to the salt solution. Due to predominant wetting, both membranes lost function­ality entirely within 40 min of operation. Given PVDF’s intrinsic hy­drophobicity and susceptibility to rapid wetting, incorporating a thin hydrophilic layer, as demonstrated in Janus membranes, can signifi­cantly improve MD membrane performance.

content: Fig. 6A and B illustrate the performance of the M5-2 and M6-2 membranes when exposed to SDS and oil-water emulsion. The M5-2 membrane showed a significant flux decline, eventually reaching negative flux within 3 h. The reduced surface tension of the liquid on the PAI layer disrupts the air-water interface stability within the hydro­phobic PVDF-SiO2 membrane pores, leading to a gradual increase in water flux and indicating signs of partial pore wetting. In contrast, M6-2 demonstrated greater resistance to SDS wetting due to its thinner hy­drophilic LIG layer, which mitigated the likelihood of pore wetting. The M6-2 membrane maintained steady rejection for the first 3 h after SDS addition but declined thereafter, reaching 76 % rejection after 6 h (Fig. 6B). This slow-wetting phenomenon is observed when SDS mole­cules can permeate the hydrophilic top layer and subsequently adsorb onto the hydrophobic substrate surface and membrane pore wall hy­drophilic surfaces [62]. Upon the addition of oil, the surface tension of the solution decreased further, resulting in a negative flux for the M5-2 membrane within 1 h due to pore wetting, as shown in Fig. 6C. While PAI is known for its underwater oleophobicity [63], this rapid wetting and pore blocking can be attributed to the elevated temperatures during MD operation. These conditions can disrupt the hydration layer [63], contributing to underwater oleophobicity by causing slight thermal expansion or reorganization in the PAI polymer.

citation.title: Electrically conductive laser-induced graphene engraved Janus membrane for membrane distillation

content: n disrupt the hydration layer [63], contributing to underwater oleophobicity by causing slight thermal expansion or reorganization in the PAI polymer. This effect destabilizes the protective water layer at the membrane-feed interface, increasing susceptibility to oil adhesion and pore blocking, as shown in Fig. 6C. In contrast, the M6-2 membrane, with its LIG-modified surface, prevented rapid pore blocking. It demonstrated a slight increase in flux due to the higher SDS concentration at the critical micelle concentration (CMC) and enhanced mobility resulting from the presence of oil in the hot feed water (Fig. 6D). The water vapor flux increased initially from 11 LMH in the first hour to 13 LMH in the second hour, followed by a gradual decline. Notably, the rejection performance of the M6-2 membrane did decrease rapidly, as observed in Fig. 6B, indicating that while SDS pri­marily contributes to pore wetting, it does not significantly compromise rejection. These findings highlight the enhanced durability of the LIG-decorated membrane, which can withstand low surface tension liquids and maintain functionality over extended operation. The study further examines the application of direct current (DC) at an electric potential of 3V in both cathodic and anodic modes to evaluate the antifouling performance of the M6-2 membrane. Under a cathodic voltage (-3V), the membrane exhibited a flux decline rate (FDR) of 2.45 % per hour during 6 h of continuous operation (Fig. 6E).

content: ane. Under a cathodic voltage (-3V), the membrane exhibited a flux decline rate (FDR) of 2.45 % per hour during 6 h of continuous operation (Fig. 6E). This suggests that the cathodic potential effectively repels oil droplets, minimizing hydrophobic interactions and fouling. The non-polar dodecyl tail (C12H25) of SDS attached to the oil and the negatively charged sulfate (SO4-) group at the head remains in the aqueous feed. While the hy­drophobic oil can be prevented by the hydration layer generated on the LIG surface, a negative electric potential can repel the SDS head, reducing the wetting effect. Thus, the functional hydrophilic electrically conductive LIG layer can be tuned according to the presence of charged particles in the feed, improving the anti-wetting properties of the Janus matrix. Conversely, applying an anodic potential (+3V) resulted in a much higher FDR of 12.4 % per hour (Fig. 6F), as the positively charged membrane attracted the negatively charged oil droplets. The behavior of the M6-2 membrane under these conditions is driven by electrostatic attraction and repulsion mechanisms, as well as electrophoretic mixing. The oil droplets, emulsified with an anionic SDS surfactant, carry a negative charge. Under a cathodic potential, these droplets are repelled from the membrane surface, reducing fouling and flux decline. In contrast, an anodic potential attracts the negatively charged oil droplets, exacerbating fouling and flux reduction.

Additionally, the applied voltage induces an electrophoretic force that displaces charged oil droplets away from the membrane surface, further enhancing anti­fouling performance. These results demonstrate the potential of the M62 membrane’s electroactive properties to improve its resistance to fouling in feed water containing charged contaminants, particularly under cathodic operation.

content: 3.2.3. Mineral scaling evaluation under different electric field conditions To investigate the role of the electric field in mitigating mineral scale (inorganic salt) formation, we evaluated membrane performance under various operational conditions, including no applied potential, direct current (DC), and alternating current (AC). In DC mode, a voltage of -1 V was applied to the M6-2 membrane, while in AC experiments, a 1 V potential was tested using square and sinusoidal waveforms at three frequencies (1, 5, and 10 Hz). Fig. 7A illustrates the normalized water flux of the M6-2 membrane with a hypersaline monovalent salt solution (200,000 mg/L NaCl). Mineral scaling in the MD process progresses through three main stages [47,64,65], as illustrated in Fig. S9: (1) a slow flux decline due to limited heterogeneous nucleation on the membrane surface, (2) a rapid flux drop caused by homogeneous nucleation in the bulk solution, and (3) a near-zero flux stage with increased distillate conductivity, indicating significant scaling and reduced separation performance. Under no-potential conditions, the M6-2 membrane exhibited these distinct scaling stages, with a gradual flux decline during the first 2 h, followed by a rapid drop, reaching zero flux at 6 h (Fig. 7A). When a cathodic potential of -1 V was applied, the resulting negative charge repelled positively charged scaling ions, disrupting the nucleation pro­cess and reducing scale deposition.

citation.title: Electrically conductive laser-induced graphene engraved Janus membrane for membrane distillation

content: was applied, the resulting negative charge repelled positively charged scaling ions, disrupting the nucleation pro­cess and reducing scale deposition. This extended the heterogeneous nucleation phase from 120 min to over 150 min, delaying the onset of homogeneous nucleation. The flux decline under a -1V DC potential proceeded more gradually, reaching zero after 360 min. The applied potential generates an electrical double layer (EDL) at the membrane surface, where counter-ions accumulate and co-ions are depleted [66]. This EDL reduces the local saturation index, slowing nucleation and mitigating mineral scaling. Additionally, the electric field redirected scaling species into the bulk solution, minimizing adhesion and scale layer formation on the membrane. In some cases, the DC potential could also induce electrolysis reactions, resulting in local pH changes that further inhibit scale formation by altering the solubility and precipitation behavior of scaling ions [67]. Numerous studies have reported that effective mitigation of inor­ganic scaling can be achieved through the application of an AC electric field [66,67]. Several mechanisms are explained to validate the effect of AC electrical field in mitigating the mineral scale formation as follows [68]: 1. Minimizing concentration polarization (CP): The constantly changing polarity of an AC electric field helps reduce the CP effect by periodically reversing the charge at the membrane surface.

citation.title: Electrically conductive laser-induced graphene engraved Janus membrane for membrane distillation

content: CP): The constantly changing polarity of an AC electric field helps reduce the CP effect by periodically reversing the charge at the membrane surface. This dynamic pre­vents ions from accumulating near the surface, which is crucial for minimizing scaling, as ion buildup often leads to crystallization and scale formation.2. Disturbance of the EDL: As opposed to the DC electric field, AC alternated the polarity of the surface, thereby disturbing the EDL and preventing stable nucleation of scale-forming ions like Ca+ and SO42-. The dynamic nature of an AC electric field makes it more challenging for ions to form stable deposits, unlike DC conditions where a stable electric field promotes ion buildup. This constant shift in polarity disrupts ion interactions near the membrane surface, reducing the likelihood of stable scale formation.3. Electrophoretic mixing and surface turbulence: Electrophoretic mixing induced by rapid reversal of current direction causes local turbu­lence, helping in dispersing ions away from the membrane surface, reducing the likelihood of ion clusters forming and adhering, which can significantly mitigate scaling [66].4. No localized pH changes: The dynamic AC environment also prevents localized pH changes, unlike the cathodic DC environment, where a stable negative potential can raise local pH and promote the pre­cipitation of certain minerals.

content: To evaluate the effect of AC electric field application, we monitored the effect of frequency (1, 5, and 10 Hz) and two common AC wave­forms, including sinusoidal and square, on the monovalent and multi­valent ion solutions. The frequency of AC means the rate at which AC changes per second, explaining how many times the reversal happens when applied to an electrically conductive surface. The waveform of AC refers to the shape of AC, showing how it alternates, changing direction and amplitude. For a sine wave, the voltage rises and falls smoothly, changing between positive and negative peaks periodically, while square waveform switches abruptly between maximum positive and maximum negative values(see Fig. S10A). Following this periodic switch, oppositely charged ions move toward the membrane surface, causing electrophoretic movement and preventing the accumulation and nucleation of ions (Fig. S10B). In Fig. 7A, we investigated the role of 1 V AC voltage in sine and square waveforms for monovalent ion solu­tion using peak-to-peak voltage. The wave oscillates between +0.5V and -0.5V, making the total range 1 V. For 1 Hz frequency AC waveforms, we see a huge improvement in the flux compared to no electric field (no DC) and DC (-1V) conditions. For both sine and square waveforms, the heterogenous nucleation stage was found to be predominant, and the flux decline showed slow progress. The square waveform showed gradual flux reduction, reaching an FDR of 2.7 % per hour.

content: nd to be predominant, and the flux decline showed slow progress. The square waveform showed gradual flux reduction, reaching an FDR of 2.7 % per hour. Meanwhile, the sine waveform at 1 Hz frequency shows a gradual flux decline (FDR 2.7 % per hour) until 280 min, followed by a rapid flux decline, making the FDR 15.94 % per hour (Fig. 7A). Such an observed phenomenon can be due to the effect of electrophoretic movement, which is insufficient for complete scaling mitigation. At 5 Hz, we see a gradual flux decline for both sine and square waveforms, showing FDR of 1.5 % and 0.93 % per hour, respectively. The frequent abrupt switching of the square waveform is effective for monovalent ion scaling prevention. At 10 Hz, the dramatic flux decline highlights that the excessive electrochemical interaction can accelerate the scaling formation process. The less frequent reversing also optimally disrupts the EDL, preventing ion buildup stability near the membrane surface. Due to the complex chemistry of multivalent ions, flux decline begins earlier than with monovalent ions, reflecting the higher scaling tendency of ions such as Ca2+, CO32-, and SO42-. As seen in Fig. 7B, multivalent ions exhibit a quicker scaling tendency, driven by their electrostatic interactions in the presence of an electric field. When Na+ and Cl-ions are mixed with multivalent ions, the heterogeneous nucleation stage is eliminated under a 1 Hz AC fre­quency. Interestingly, at 1 Hz, the sine waveform slows flux decline, maintaining flux until zero is reached within the 6-h experimental window, whereas the square waveform struggles to control electro­phoretic movement. The strong electrostatic interactions of multivalent ions necessitate gradual field changes to prevent accumulation; the abrupt polarity shifts of the square waveform inhibit ion relaxation, allowing clustering. Consequently, the sine waveform at 1 Hz out­performs the square waveform. At 5 Hz, however, the square waveform provides better ion control, resulting in a more gradual flux decline, indicative of a sustained het­erogeneous nucleation stage, with flux declining at an FDR of 6 % per hour.

At 10 Hz, the rapid polarity shifts of both waveforms produce a rapid flux decline, although the sine waveform demonstrates slightly better ion control. In all experiments, permeate conductivity and salt rejection were moni­tored, revealing that M6-2 maintains salt rejection above 99 % across conditions, except for a slight drop to 97.5 % in the multivalent salt solution with no electric field, likely due to scale formation on the membrane. These findings highlight the potential of AC electric fields in reducing scaling with brine solutions containing both monovalent and multivalent salts and underscore the importance of optimizing AC waveform and frequency. The electrical conductivity of the membrane M6-2 was measured after a 6-h operation under both 3V DC and 1V AC potentials, as presented in Table S6, showing that the membrane’s conductivity remained stable at approximately 142 ± 1.5 S/cm, with no significant changes observed after 6 h of operation under the tested conditions.

To evaluate the long-term performance of the M6-2 membrane, an 18-h test was conducted under the optimized AC test condition of 5 Hz/ 1 V (square wave) using a monovalent salt solution containing 200,000 mg/L NaCl. Fig. S11 illustrates the normalized flux over time, revealing a minimal flux decline after each 6-h run. The flux decline rate (FDR) was 0.93 %/hr during the first 6 h, increasing to 1.16 %/hr after 12 h and 1.35 %/hr after 18 h. These findings highlight the potential of applying AC potential for mitigating inorganic salt scaling over extended periods, outperforming scenarios with no electric field or DC potential. Additionally, the surface morphology and elemental composition of the M6-2 membrane before and after the long-term test were analyzed using FESEM and EDS. As shown in Fig. S12, the surface morphology of the LIG structure remained unchanged, and no salt deposition was detected after cleaning the membrane with cross-circulating water. In contrast, traces of Na (3.42 %) and Cl (2.74 %) were observed without cleaning. Overall, the results demonstrate that the membrane’s flux can be effectively restored through simple cleaning, and its morphology re­mains stable even when exposed to high concentrations of salt solution during long-term operation. 4.

Conclusion This study successfully developed a novel membrane distillation (MD) membrane with an in-situ conductive layer created from laserinduced graphene (LIG) engraved on polyamide-imide (PAI), reported here for the first time. An independent hydrophobic structure was then fabricated to create a multifunctional membrane. This approach over­comes the high porosity challenges of LIG structures while avoiding coating techniques that typically reduce membrane flux. Optimized membrane parameters achieved a flux of 11.3 ± 0.56 LMH and a rejection rate of 99.77 ± 0.1 %, making it well-suited for MD operations and comparable to values reported in the literature. The membrane exhibited a high electrical conductivity of 142 ± 1.5 S/cm, confirmed through electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) analyses. Applying an electric field significantly improved the membrane’s resistance to wetting and pore-blocking, even with a critical micelle concentration (0.5 mM SDS) of surfactant solution and a 1000 ppm nhexadecane oil-water emulsion. Additionally, cathodic and anodic DC potentials verified the membrane’s electrochemical activity under an applied electric field.

citation.title: Electrically conductive laser-induced graphene engraved Janus membrane for membrane distillation

content: oil-water emulsion. Additionally, cathodic and anodic DC potentials verified the membrane’s electrochemical activity under an applied electric field. Importantly, this study explored mineral scale mitigation strategies and scaling mechanisms under both DC and AC electric fields, with a detailed analysis of AC parameters suggesting that optimizing AC frequency is crucial for preventing scaling and enhancing water treatment performance. Results indicate that 5 Hz AC outperforms 10 Hz in mitigating mineral scaling for both monovalent and multivalent salt solutions. The longer relaxation time at 5 Hz allows ions to move more effectively away from the surface, reducing nucleation. Electrophoretic mixing is more pronounced at this frequency, creating localized turbulence that pre­vents ion accumulation and prenucleation clustering. Additionally, the slower frequency optimally disrupts the electric double layer (EDL), preventing stable ion buildup near the membrane surface. In contrast, the faster switching at 10 Hz provides less time for effective ion displacement, leading to partial accumulation and increased scaling. Future studies could examine its efficiency in degrading organic con­taminants, antibiofouling properties, and optimized conditions for various contaminant feed solutions.
